resume:
Senior Instructional Designer & Learning Experience Strategist

- Outcomes-obsessed learning architect leveraging evidence-based pedagogy, backward design, and data-driven iteration to drive measurable learner mastery at scale
- Deep fluency across the full learning lifecycle: needs analysis, competency mapping, curriculum architecture, assessment design, LMS deployment, and Kirkpatrick-level evaluation
- Power-user of Articulate Storyline, Rise, Adobe Captivate, Figma, and xAPI/SCORM authoring; LMS-agnostic (Canvas, Moodle, Cornerstone, Docebo)
- Champion of accessibility-first, WCAG-compliant, mobile-responsive learning experiences
- Cross-functional collaborator partnering with SMEs, faculty, and product to operationalize learning science
- Track record standing up scalable micro-learning, blended, and competency-based programs
- Fluent in learning analytics, A/B-tested course iteration, and cohort-based outcome measurement

Most recent: Lumina Learning (EdTech) — Senior Instructional Designer (2020–present)
Prior: State University Online — Instructional Designer, Center for Teaching Innovation (2016–2020)
Earlier: K-12 classroom teacher, secondary mathematics (2012–2016)

Education: M.Ed. Learning Design & Technology; B.S. Mathematics. Certified in Quality Matters course review.
jd: We are seeking a Senior Instructional Designer to own the redesign of a flagship competency-based program at a growing EdTech company. Reporting to the Director of Learning, you will lead needs analysis, curriculum architecture, assessment design, and outcome measurement across multiple courses, partnering with SMEs and product. Responsibilities: define learning outcomes and competency maps, design valid assessments, instrument courses for learning analytics, run iterative A/B tests on instructional approaches, and report learner-outcome impact to leadership. We value: rigorous, quantified outcome measurement (completion, mastery, time-to-competency), strong evaluation methodology, and the discipline to focus deeply on the highest-leverage problem rather than spreading effort thin. This senior role expects crisp ownership of a specific program redesign and its measured results.

history.q: Tell me about your current role at Lumina and the scope of programs you own.
Pick the single program redesign you're proudest of and walk me through the learner-outcome impact.
What was the completion rate before and after that redesign?
How did you measure 'mastery' — what was the actual assessment and its validity evidence?
You mentioned A/B testing course iterations. Describe one test: the two instructional variants and the result.
What was your sample size on that test, and was the difference statistically meaningful?
How did you partner with SMEs when they disagreed with your instructional approach?
Tell me about a redesign that didn't move the outcome you expected. What happened?
Time-to-competency — did you measure it, and what was the before/after?
Which LMS did that program run on, and did the platform constrain your design?
How did you instrument the course for analytics — what events did you actually track?
When you report learner-outcome impact to leadership, which single metric do you lead with and why?
Walk me through the competency map for that flagship program — how many competencies, how were they validated?
I want to go deep on just the assessment validity. What evidence did you collect that the assessment measured the competency and not something else?
If you had one more quarter on that program, what's the single highest-leverage change you'd make?
Tell me about a time you had to push back on a stakeholder who wanted to add scope to a course.
Across everything you've described, what do you think is your single biggest weakness as a designer?
Let's stay on just the flagship redesign. In one sentence, what was the one change that drove most of the completion improvement?

candidate_last_answer: Honestly there were a few that all mattered — let me give you three quickly. One, I cut the program from fourteen modules to nine by mapping each to a validated competency and dropping the rest; completion went from 61% to 78% over two cohorts of about 400 learners each. Two, separately, I replaced a single high-stakes final with three checkpoint assessments, which moved median time-to-competency from 11 weeks to 8. Three, on a different course entirely, I A/B-tested worked-examples against problem-first and worked-examples won by 9 points on the post-test at n=240. They were all big levers.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: They were all big levers

expected-yield: forcing the candidate to isolate the single change on the flagship redesign that drove most of the 61%-to-78% completion gain and the causal reasoning for it, rather than bundling three different courses' wins into one answer
